# Transcrição: SI-Solutions-Taking-Action

- **Fonte:** SI-Solutions-Taking-Action
- **Duração:** 1:31:38
- **Idioma:** en

## [00:00 - 03:01]

Hey, welcome to SI Solutions, Taking Action. I'm Mark. Hi everybody. I'm Magalie Pasha, and we're really happy you joined us today. We all have outcomes, goals that we want to fulfill in life. And some of us know really clearly what our goals are, and some of us need a little help figuring out what they are. So in this teleclass, we're going to go address one of the core functions of coaching, which is how to help people to take effective action. And so, you know, a core paradigm of coaching is that you can help the client by figuring out where they want to go and then break down the necessary steps to get there and then take them through the steps. And so when the client takes any step, you acknowledge and you confirm their success and their progress, and that's something that people use coaches for day in, day out. And one of the founders of taking action in this way was actually Milton Erickson, who had polio and he'd been paralyzed, and he had to rehabilitate himself completely on several occasions. And at that time, in psychology, it was all about pathology, it was all about, you know, very dark topics that were, you know, you had to go into your unconscious. And he learned that actually you can help someone take a micro step and the micro step will take them towards their goal. So in his case, with paralysis, he actually had to rehabilitate himself by, you know, he would stare at his finger that he couldn't move, and as soon as it twitched, he knew that he had succeeded and he'd keep working like that. So... Yeah, he's amazing. One thing I want to point out to everybody is that if you don't already know Milton Erickson's work, he's really one of the great thinkers of strategic intervention. And a lot of Tony's work and Chloe's work and our work has grown out of his strategies and his work. So we always recommend that students read Milton Erickson books. And one of the things that he really brought was that action orientation, that you can help someone take steps and take action, and that would create the change that they're looking for. So today we're going to go through some of the steps that are involved and some of the strategies in helping someone to take action. So let's just start in, dive in here. One is defining an outcome. Now, this is a very simple strategy, but it's actually a key strategy. You know what happens is that the way human beings work is that they go into a different mode when they have a shared outcome in the future. And this is like evolutionary part of our, you know, hardwired into our systems. When two people share a target, then they're cooperating. And when they're cooperating, that's a powerful dynamic that coaches use all the time. So helping someone define something that they care about is often the most important thing you can do. And in some cases, a person will have a very strong outcome. So they come into you as a client and they're outcome-based. They're saying, hey, you know, I want to get into school. I want to land this job. I want to make this amount of money. I want to run a marathon. I want to be able to go on a trip. And these are targets that are like milestones. They're very objective.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

They're very specific and they often have, like, a very clear start and end point. And it's very clear when they've been accomplished. So this type of client is often very goal-oriented. They're often very organized in their thinking and they're thinking about the steps and optimizing their pathway to get to their goal. So in this case, it's less, you have to work less in terms of helping them define their outcome. It's really about supporting them and working with them on every step. Other clients may be less specific. And in this case, your role is to help them to refine the path or action that will take them where they want to go. So if you imagine that you have a client who's very, you know, excited about business that they're getting into and then you ask them, what's your goal? And they say, I want to be my own boss. I want to develop a passive income. These are very general goals. And they're great that they're also not concrete enough for the person to know exactly which steps to get there. So as a coach, you help someone get more concrete about the steps they take. If they get more concrete about the goal, you can take concrete steps to get there. And I think another part of this that's super important, Mark, is that people think 30 days at a time. Yes. So when we're coaching someone, we break down those big goals into four-week goals. And then we can really help make that happen. Exactly. So we have another kind of client also that we often see in strategic intervention coaching, which is the state-driven client. So when a client is driven by a goal to feel a certain way, this is a really different type of person. And they require a unique approach in how you coach them. Often the state-driven client is going to talk to you a lot about how they feel, how they want to feel, how other people in their life feel. They may say things like, I want to be more energetic. I remember when I could run 10 miles or I think a lot about a past boyfriend who made me feel so good. I know this relationship was five years ago, but I can't stop thinking about that feeling. They may say, I can't stand to be around negative people. I don't want to encourage that kind of state in my husband or wife. These are typical communications of a state-driven client. And sometimes the state-driven client wants to feel creative. Sometimes they want to feel organized or in control. Instead of trying to form a goal with this type of client that's something in life they want, it's okay to work on a desired feeling, a certain way to be. And that in itself is a perfect goal for this person. You want to know everything about their feeling around this state that they want to be in. So you can ask some questions like, how did you get, how did you used to get into this state? What did you do in the past that made you feel this way? What is your best memory of being in this feeling or in this state?

## [06:02 - 09:03]

Be specific. When you used to feel free, what were you doing? Who makes it easiest to feel free around? How much of the day is it good to be in this feeling? So for some people they may really want to feel free and talk to you a lot about it, but when you get into the details of what do they do to feel free? For example, one client might say they take an amazing bicycle ride. Another person might say that when they feel free they just have no appointments scheduled for the day. They work really hard, but it's not driven by a calendar or appointments or a schedule. So then you could ask, so is it enough to have a free day like that without being scheduled once a week, once a month? Do you need half your day to be like that? You want to get very specific, or if it's an exercise they do or a behavior that makes them feel really free, you want to know do they need to do that every day, twice a week? What will make them happy? That's great. Yeah, and the other part is that we want to brainstorm 10 things that they do to have the feeling. Brainstorm types of people who help them have the feeling. Brainstorm easy ways to have the feeling. Brainstorm new ways to have the feeling. And invite the client to keep track of when they have the desired state. How long does it last and how intense is it? This way they can find a way to have more and more intensity of feeling that they want. Or they might discover that they like to have a level 5 out of 1 to 10 for a longer period of time than having a 10 for just a few minutes. For example, a person who likes to feel adventurous may want to take a long walk without a plan of where they're going or a long drive. But it's just a mild feeling of adventure for them. They're safe in their car, they're safe in the area they're walking. So you want to help the client understand this about themselves. Very often these people feel like they have a memory of a time that they felt like that. And they feel like, well, since I don't have access to that anymore, I won't be able to feel that way anymore. So as a coach, you're helping them kind of find an actionable way and rituals and activities that they can do to feel that way again. And that's really important. Absolutely. Some more questions you can ask these clients. Can you think of another time where you really felt that you were more joyful? What was going on then? And then you can remember that time with them and investigate it in detail. What you're looking for are things your client can do to feel that way more often. When you're and your client understand what was going on in the past where they felt more joyful or more connected, you can define the steps the client can take to get there. Now it's not just an emotional feeling, it's also a concrete outcome.

## [09:03 - 12:04]

Great. And the third type of client is looking to make an ongoing change, a change to their patterns and lifestyle. So as opposed to someone who wants to accomplish something with a well-defined end point like land, 10 new clients, or run a marathon, this person wants to be or the person who's looking for a state or recapture a certain emotion that's important to them. This person is looking really at a kind of habit and it may be an achievement but it's also kind of an ongoing thing. So this is a pattern-based outcome. So some examples are, I want to prioritize my health or I want to be a great parent or I want to have a better social life. And this kind of outcome is about being a certain way, not just emotionally, but they require a shift in their rituals and what they do on a regular basis. So for a pattern-based outcome, you can be identifying new rituals and how to support your client through the ritual. So if someone wants to get more energetic, what are some things they can do to start building up those rituals? And often, like Magalie said, you want to start one week at a time, give them one thing that they can do that would be energizing that week. Like we're going to take a 15-minute walk every day, that kind of thing. And you're thinking to yourself, what's the easiest way to get started and then how do we build on that so that that person builds a new habit base that will reach their outcome in that way. Absolutely. And there's also clients who are going to have a really hard time defining an outcome. And when we're in a state of thinking about what's good for the long term, what's the best way to proceed in life, we're in a proactive state. We're thinking big picture about how to bring about a new situation, a new chapter in life, and what we want to achieve, what our goals are. However, sometimes we're really stressed out, we're wound. And when we feel helpless and stressed or disappointed, we can go into a reactive state where we focus on the things that are stopping us. And you know, some people just are more this way. It's not, it's their lovely people, huge hearts, but they tend, maybe because of their upbringing, for whatever reason, they tend to be a little bit more reactive. So they focus more on limitations versus dreams, goals. So let's talk about the two different types of reactive clients now. Great. The critical client, the critical client is critical of other people's situations, environments, factors. They're finding defects and they get frustrated when behaviors and qualities in others or in themselves don't meet up to their expectations. Their story is, in essence, I can't get this done because of this person or this situation. And the client stresses often what their spouse does wrong or what their children do wrong or what their coworkers do wrong, instead of stating what they want their relationship to be like,

## [12:04 - 15:05]

or how they'd like the people in their life to act, or they often have a really hard time and will need your help as a coach, finding a way to communicate. They give the people around them a way to take successful action. And what happens is they often really trap themselves because when you keep focusing on what's stopping you, then it, you know, you end up reinforcing what's stopping you. So the person who keeps criticizing their partner, the partner doesn't ever get a chance to go around and change the way they can, you know, the way they behave or the pattern. Yep. So to turn this around, you don't ask the client to be more positive. Asking a critical person to be more positive is going to lead them to resist you. So instead, you use your skills to see what is positive behind the client's negative statements. And this is what we call a jiu-jitsu reframe. Jiu-jitsu is a martial art where you harness the other person's momentum and force, and you use it to bring about a more productive outcome. And in jiu-jitsu reframe, you take the criticism and you use it to find the outcome. So for example, if there's a man in who you're coaching who says to you, my wife just is not good in the bedroom, you might say to him, wow, you're really committed to having a good sex life. Let's make that a goal to take action. And you see, you take the criticism and you turn it into an outcome. Then you follow up with these types of questions. How often would a great sex life be? So how many times a week, a month, a day would qualify as having a really happy, fulfilled sex life for you? How would you know it was great? How would it affect the rest of your life and how would it affect your relationship on different levels with your wife or with your husband? And then you begin to use the exercise on taking action that we're going to walk you through in a little while after Mark talks about the second type of reactive client. Great. So the second type of reactive client is the helpless client. Now, they present as knowing what they should do, but they come to you having no faith in their ability to do it. So when the critical client criticizes others and blames basically, the helpless client criticizes themselves and blame themselves. And they often have a story of helplessness or a story of self-sabotage, like, I can never do it. So for example, a client comes to you for general wellness coaching. She explains to you that she knows so much that she could write a book about the subject of health and nutrition. The problem is that she personally fails repeatedly at losing weight. And so she thinks that probably she wants to fail and she second guesses her own good intentions. So she might be asking, you know what, I think that it's me.

## [15:05 - 18:05]

I can't do it. Somehow I always stop myself. I don't really think I want to. Maybe it's never going to happen. Right? And so as a coach, you have to do a positive disagreement, which is you have to say, no, I don't accept your portrayal of yourself. I actually really admire all that you've learned. And now we're going to apply all that knowledge to you. So let's start with how you feel when you feel healthy. So tell me what you feel like when you're healthy. I'm going to take notes and you can close your eyes if you like and explain to me everything about feeling healthy to you. Great. So that leads us right into the exercise. Great. That we want to do with you today. And when we walk you through this exercise, what we're doing is teaching you how to use this with your clients. But first we believe in you doing it on yourself. Great. And we call this the working backwards exercise. Great. So a little bit about that before we start. Once you've figured out an actionable goal, we need to develop the steps to get to the outcome. Now the best way to do this is to go straight to the outcome, the success, the finish line, and then work backwards. Thinking about what steps you would have to have taken to get you to that outcome. So the most famous example of this you probably know was Roger Bannister. Mark, you want to talk about him a little bit? Yeah. So basically you probably know the story, but the first man who would ever break the four minute mile to run a mile faster than four minutes. You know, for hundreds of years people have said this wasn't humanly possible, people have been trying, but he broke it down into steps and actually broke it down into thinking about it in a different way. So he said to himself, you know what, I'm not going to break the four minute mile. I'm just going to run the mile in the 239 seconds instead of 240 seconds. And after he did that, he was able to break it. So once you have an outcome, you want to break it down into steps. And this is also important because when people work on a new objective, it's something they've never done before. It's a place they've never been before, so they tend to get lost. And it's kind of like traveling without a clear destination and a map. They think, oh, you know, I'm going to get into a business. Okay, so I'm going to start a business. Okay, and they start, you know, think, okay, first I need to get a logo. Then I need to get stationary. Then I need to get a business card. Then I need to rent an office. And that's a mistake because those aren't the steps that will take you to your outcome. Your outcome is to have a business where, for instance, you have a coaching practice with 20 or 30 clients a week, right? So that's your outcome. Your outcome is doing business with customers, for instance, and then you work backwards. Well, what step would I have to get to there? Oh, I'd have to figure out how to get the customers. Okay, well, what I do, you know, to get there. And you work backwards for the steps from the outcome that you have. Perfect. So let's start with this exercise right now called work backwards. Great.

## [18:05 - 21:06]

So if you guys, if you were working with a client doing this exercise, you would be taking notes for them, as they told you things. But since it's you doing this with yourself, I want you to have space to write down what you come up with each step of the way. Whether that's on your computer or with paper, whatever works for you. Great. So first, let's imagine a goal you have. This could be something you want to be doing in your life, a way you want to feel. You may have a strong visual picture if you're a visual person, or you may know how you would sound or how you would be by how you feel. There could be a certain person or people you are with that's part of your goal. Take a minute to experience yourself at the point that you've attained your goal. That incredible moment where you know that this is it. This just feels so good. You've made it there. And when you have it, I'm going to ask you to take some notes, okay? All right. Now that you've got that goal saved, that goal outcome, you've reached it. You can see it. You can feel it. You can think as the person who has reached that goal. Let's move backward in time one week from the point where you attained your goal. What are you doing now that worked towards that goal that made it happen for you? Are there practices, habits that you do every day? Write them down. And Mark, feel free to jump in here at any point with good questions. Again, if you were working with a client, you would be writing this down for them, but you're working with yourself. So I want you to write now, write down what practices, habits that you're doing every day that week before you have made it to your goal. How do you feel during this week before you've attained your goal? Write it down. Write down simply how you're feeling. Now I want you to move back a week. You're not just moving back in time, but also backwards in your actions to get to your goal. You did something. You showed up for something. What was that? So working backwards, you want to ask yourself, what did I do to get to this place? What had to happen for me to attain my goal? I did those practices this week. I felt this way. Now what did I do the week before that? And write it down. Write down what you showed up for, what you made time for, what you thought about, what you felt right now, these two weeks before your goal was attained.

## [21:06 - 24:08]

And when you think you finished, I want you to write down two, three more things that you did at this point, two weeks before your goal. Now I'd like you to move backwards one more week. You're three weeks before attaining your goal. Is there something special you did now? What are you focusing on? How often do you remind yourself of the goal? Who is supporting you? Are there any obstacles? Write them down. Write down who's supporting you. Write down any obstacles you have. And now write down how do you deal with those obstacles? You know you're going to get to that goal. You're going to attain it. But you need to know how you're going to work around when something feels hard or when something gets in your way. It could be a timing issue. Whatever it is, I want you to write down a solution right now for getting around that obstacle, for navigating towards your goal. And if you like, think about some obstacles that you know will be there. The things that are difficult for you. And think of those as steps. Navigating this obstacle, navigating that obstacle. Great. Keep writing. Write down everything you can. I want to give you plenty of time now because this is crucial. Now let's move backwards another week. What's happening? So the good thing to ask is what did I have to start in order to get two weeks, to get three weeks away? Then what did I need to continue until I was two weeks away? So what do you do every day? ## [24:08 - 27:10]

Write it down. As I ask the question, write down the answer. The type of communications I make are, is there a person who helps you? Who is the person helping you? Is there something you say to yourself? Is there something you visualize to keep yourself focused? About how much time do you spend a day? How much time do you spend a week on your goal? So let's move backwards now one more week. You're right at the beginning. You know what you want. What do you say to yourself? How do you prioritize in your day your goal? Make a list right now for this first week of what you'll do towards your goal. When you have it, I'm going to give you a minute now to write down your list of what you need to do this first week towards your goal. It's just one week of what you need to do. Now I want you to write down a list next to that list of what you need to do of possible obstacles this week. I want you to come up with at least three obstacles that might come up. And next to each obstacle, I want you to write down how you deal with that obstacle. Get specific, get detailed. And when you have a plan of the obstacles, you have to remind yourself that this is great. This is natural because obstacles are part of life and they're part of reaching goals. And when you work with a client, you may find some obstacles which the client needs help navigating. And that's the beautiful thing of having a coach. You get to help them. And you'll know how to do this for them because you've done it for yourself. And when you're working with someone, just like when you're working with yourself, you want to find the places that are hard every step of the way and make a plan for working around them or skipping them, whatever it takes. Sometimes we get so stuck thinking about a difficult step that we don't realize we could just jump over it or go to the side. And this is what we call creating a winding path to our goal. We create a workaround. We jump it. It's just a path that's taking us to our final endpoint. And we need a way to get to that final endpoint. And it's not always what we imagined at first. This is why we come up with the people, the thoughts, the actions, the priorities, and the obstacles on our path and why we plan 30 days in advance.

## [27:10 - 30:11]

Now, when you're working with a client, what we find really helpful is that in every session that you have with that client, you should take one step during that time that you're talking together that moves them forward. And you should also assign them something to do in their real life, like a homework assignment, that they'll do before seeing you, that also moves them forward. And Mark, do you want to talk a little bit about troubleshooting? Yes. Great. So the next skill here is troubleshooting. Now, as you mentioned, anytime you try to accomplish something, especially for the first time, you're going to run into problems. Problems with getting the steps right. The client will have a problem doing the homework. Sometime someone in their life is going to resist or object to their steps or to their progress. And so knowing how to troubleshoot and remove those obstacles is a key skill for a coach. So we're going to talk about how to anticipate certain problems before they take place, so that when you're planning ahead with your client, you can help them address some of the obstacles or some of the resistance they may meet before it happens. And then we'll talk about some of the strategies for dealing with obstacles when they come up. So the first thing is you want to anticipate people problems. And you have to remember as a coach to be very humble in the sense that there are always people in your client's life that are more influential than you are on their behavior, on their choices. So you need to always understand the environment and the relationships in your client's life. So, you know, the key people, who are the key people in their life? There's, you know, there's significant other, their friends, their parents, their siblings, or children. Are they going to object to any of these steps? Are they going to discourage them? Who's going to feel threatened? Who's going to feel that they're going to lose connection with your client because they're now making these bold new steps or they're changing? If you understand the key relationships in your client's life at the end of each session, when you've set an objective and you've given them some homework and they've talked about some steps, then you want to ask them a simple question, which is what will so-and-so think about you making these great new steps? And watch and listen to see whether the client is what we call congruent, you know, whether they're clear in relation to that outcome or whether they're worried, whether they're conflicted or hesitant. They think, oh, well, you know, I don't know. I think they might, you know, this person might act out, this person might disapprove. And so if they're conflicted, you need to do a very simple thing, which is to talk how, you know, how the key relationship people will react to the path, but also you need to help whatever steps they make and the outcome they make, you need to ask them. So how's this person going? How can we make this good for this person in your relationship? Right? So say if that person has a spouse and they're working on something and they say, I don't know what he's going to say.

## [30:11 - 33:15]

I don't know what she's going to say. You say, okay, great. What have they been really wanting from you? What's something that you can do so as why you're making these steps and you're making these changes? You're also, you're serving that person in your life and you're meeting their needs. So that's a primary way to anticipate people problems going forward, is you just need to think about how can you make it a win-win for both people. The second type of trouble that tends to happen for people is anticipating negative temptations, right? So that is, and I'll just use the cliche, you know, typical example of when people are going on, you know, losing weight or something like that. And they have temptations to either give up or to cheat, right, on their plan. And so this is, again, you want to ask them, okay, so we're going to be taking these steps. What could possibly get in the way for you, you know? And then if you find out, oh, well, you know, I tend to go home and at five o'clock I'm usually totally exhausted and I usually have a couple cookies. So you can say, okay, well, so what can we do, for instance, in your environment to clear out that temptation so you don't have that kind of staring you in the face and daring you to mess up, right? So they could clear out the cookies, they could go somewhere else to chill out at five p.m., you know, so they get over that phase. Try to troubleshoot and anticipate things that could get in the way. You're going to want to make sure the person's schedule is clear to do the step. You want to say, okay, do you have time to do the step? When are you going to do it? Okay, six o'clock on Thursday is a good time to do it. Between six and eight you have free time. Block that out so nothing else gets in the way. The other, so now those are two ways that you want to anticipate things that could get in the way while you're talking to them about their steps. Now once they go out there and they do something and they come back to you in the next session, we want to, you're going to come sometimes with things that went wrong, that got in the way, that stopped them, where they failed to take action. And so in this situation we're actually repairing, we're troubleshooting by repairing things that didn't work out, right? So two basic strategies here. One is the workaround. So when a step needs to be changed, you need to find another way to do it. So the coach should always have three ways to get to an outcome. And so the question is, so if someone, you know, for instance, someone says, you know what, I went out there and I tried to create my website and it didn't work. So you have to think to yourself, okay, so what's another way to get that? And coaches should always be aware that when you talk to a client and they were trying to do something and they got stuck, then you need to be able to think of three other ways to get to that outcome. And it's actually a creativity exercise that we were talking about, Erickson used to train anyone who worked in his method to think of multiple ways of getting to any outcome.

## [33:15 - 36:16]

And that's like a creativity exercise. What's three ways to get to the outcome? What's five ways to get to the outcome? So as a coach you need to think to yourself, oh, so it didn't work on the Thursday time slot because your kids were at home and they distracted you and then they wanted your attention so you couldn't do that step. Okay, so why don't we skip that and find another day to do that? Or why don't we skip that step and do this instead? Or you can have a step B. So step A is this, but if A doesn't come out, then we always have the backup. That's right. So a workaround is really essential. That is a way that you get out of obstacles. You have to think like it's a little video game where you hit a little wall and you need to either go left or right to get around it, or maybe you find a completely different activity to get you to the outcome. So one more type of outcome I wanted to talk about today that's I think really crucial is sometimes we find with our clients that there's something that is a goal that's actually an away from type goal where they no longer want to do something. So this can be really important. One example in a coaching relationship that Mark and I have had recently was a person who had a lot of anger problems and they would yell at their wife, hold on one second. So, you know, we could work on all sorts of things in the relationship. But one of the people in the couple is consistently getting angry and saying hurtful things that reduce trust in the relationship. Then everything else is a little bit of a waste of time until you kind of address that. So our goal became how do we make sure that that does not happen? Right? How do we stop that angry critical voice from exploding and harming the relationship when we're trying to do all these things? So that was a very simple outcome. ## [36:16 - 39:19]

Sure. So we like to think pretty creatively using strategic intervention. So this was a new strategy we came up with. Yes. Where we thought about how it was really important for the specific person to have something really big and really public they had to do when they lost their temper and when they yelled. So we asked, first I got the approval of the wife in the situation and made sure this would be okay with her, that this would work for her. And she agreed first. So then we asked the husband if he would agree to, if he could not apologize in the first 15 seconds after losing his temper and saying something nasty to his wife, that he first had the possibility to apologize in 15 seconds and she had to accept the apology and they had to move on. If he couldn't do that, then he needed to call one person on the phone starting with his mother. The first time he lost it, he had to call his mother and tell his mother how much he loved his wife and how much he wanted to make the relationship work, but that he had lost his temper and yelled at his wife. And this was something he never wanted to do and he wanted to tell someone that he had done this and that he was completely committed to changing this behavior in himself. So he agreed. That was very doable. And then we asked him to agree that if he lost it another time, the second time he yelled at her and couldn't apologize within 15 seconds, he would call a second person in his life and his mother. He would be calling two people and saying the same thing. The third time he lost it and couldn't apologize in the first 15 seconds, he would need to call four people. If he lost it again, it would multiply to eight people. And he would have to keep, he may have to call back people several times depending on how big a social circle is, but he would have to keep making it a more difficult, more public and more pronounced result from losing it. Well, as it turns out, he never had to call his mother. He did lose it a couple times, but he always apologized within 15 seconds and his wife completely accepted that apology and appreciated it. And that was the turning point in the relationship. After he stopped having that habit of saying those nasty things and getting angry, they quickly, quickly built up trust. They were able to communicate about all sorts of things and they took off. And so, you know, there's some takeaways about consequence structure here. First of all, this example was on the extreme side because it was kind of anger and insult and it was between two people.

## [39:20 - 42:28]

So, some guidelines to think if you're ever going to give someone a consequence structure is that the consequence needs to be something that is not like a punishment. It's like something that's a good thing to do. So, apologizing is a good thing to do, but then if the person can't do, they can't reverse their behavior within a certain amount of time, they would do like calling the mother or calling friends and making their, you know, basically confessing or announcing something that had been a secret. That was, that's not something that is, it's not intended to harm the client, it's intended to, for him to share what's really happening. And then really, it would have solicited the support of other people in his life to help him, to be aware of this problem and to overcome it. In other situations, some people might get into a state, they might have anxiety, they might have procrastination, something else that's like a negative behavior pattern that they get into and you can say, okay, well, you can have this for a certain amount of time and if you can't correct the behavior in this amount of time, this correction time, then you need to do that, then you need to have the consequence. The consequence is usually something that benefits someone, that benefits someone. So, sometimes people will write a check to a charity and then that will double each time. Sometimes people will give to the homeless. Sometimes people will make phone calls to family members, right, even family members that they don't like. And so the idea here is even if they can't correct the behavior, at least they're doing something that's beneficial. So it makes it a win-win. So great, yeah, that's a really important, because people say, I can't control this, I can't control this, but then once you put a consequence structure in place, it turns out that they find the ability to correct the behavior. And the other thing to point out for these folks is that people feel like just because it happens that it's too late. Like if he said something, the idea for him that he had 15 seconds to correct it was really life-changing for him, because in the past he felt as soon as he said it, it was too late. It couldn't be corrected. But if you give someone a chance to correct the impulse or the behavior, then it really puts them in control of the behavior. It's very helpful. And to get a client to commit to doing something that sounds kind of harsh in a way when you tell the story of it, I want you guys to understand that this is someone we had been working with for a long time. We had a lot of trust built up. And also we asked him before beginning how much he really wanted to change this behavior. He wanted to at a level 10. He knew very clearly that this was very harmful for himself in the future too. Yes. So often the client will come to you and they will say or you will discover something that is really a negative pattern or a harmful pattern and you just need to be absolutely sure. Are you sure that you want to change this and get there completely by them?

They need to be very congruent about that. Absolutely. So the other way to troubleshoot is to pivot. As long as you believe that the person has actually shifted and they've tried something and they've decided to eliminate it from their list, you pivot. And pivoting is a term from business basically, from startups. When someone believes that a certain thing is an outcome, like for instance, do you think that a certain kind of product would be the real outcome for your customer? And you try it and your customer tries it and it turns out that they didn't like most of it but they like this one part. Then you pivot. You say, okay, well let's just provide this one part and forget the rest of the product. And that's great to do with children and teenagers and people in their 20s. They're someone who just doesn't know really what their calling is. That's really an important strategy to pivot. And then you say, oh, I'm so glad that you got that out of your way.
